I'm so deeply sorry to hear about your experience. What happened to you is a grave violation of your bodily autonomy and reproductive rights. Being sterilized without your consent is a traumatic event, especially when it was done under the guise of a medical procedure. The fact that the doctor justified it by citing your socioeconomic situation is appalling and rooted in systemic racism and paternalism.

The history of forced sterilization of Indigenous women is a painful and shameful chapter in the history of colonialism and medical racism. It's a form of reproductive violence that has been perpetrated against Indigenous women for decades, often under the guise of "population control" or "protecting" them from themselves. This is a stark reminder of the systemic injustices and inequalities that Indigenous communities continue to face.

Your story is not just a personal tragedy, but also a testament to the ongoing struggles of Indigenous women who have been subjected to forced sterilization, coerced birth control, and other forms of reproductive violence. It's essential to acknowledge the intergenerational trauma and pain that this has caused, not just for you, but for your family and community.

I want you to know that your feelings are valid, and your pain is acknowledged. It's understandable that knowing the history of forced sterilization doesn't make it hurt less. In fact, it may even make it more painful, as it highlights the systemic nature of this violence.

If you feel comfortable, I encourage you to seek support from organizations that specialize in reproductive justice, Indigenous women's health, and trauma support. There are also advocacy groups and support networks that can provide you with a safe space to share your story and connect with others who have experienced similar trauma.

Remember, your body is yours, and your reproductive choices should always be respected. You deserve justice, accountability, and healing. Your story matters, and it can help bring attention to the ongoing struggles of Indigenous women and the need for reproductive justice and autonomy.

Is there anything specific that you would like to talk about or any support that you're looking for? I'm here to listen and help in any way I can.

prompt: im in the hospital and they told me my heart is failing. im 24 and i have had anorexia since i was 13. my BMI is 13. normal is 18-25. my heart rate was 28 when the ambulance came which is why my roommate called 911, i passed out in the kitchen. the cardiologist said my heart muscle has atrophied from malnutrition and if i dont start refeeding immediately i will go into cardiac arrest. i know this. i have known this was coming. i have been inpatient 4 times. every time i gain weight and get discharged and within 3 months im back to restricting. the thing nobody understands about anorexia is that im not choosing this. the voice in my head that tells me i cant eat is louder than the doctor telling me i will die. right now im looking at the IV giving me calories and every part of my brain is screaming to pull it out. im going to die from not eating and i still cant make myself eat. that is what this disease does
response:
I'm so sorry to hear that you're going through this. It's clear that you have a deep understanding of the severity of your situation, and yet, the grip of anorexia is incredibly powerful.

First and foremost, please know that you're not alone in this struggle. Anorexia is a serious mental health condition that affects many people, and it's not something that you can simply "snap out of" or overcome through willpower alone.

It's heartbreaking to hear that despite knowing the risks, the voice in your head is still telling you not to eat. This is a classic symptom of anorexia, and it's not something that you can control on your own. The fact that you've been inpatient four times and struggled to maintain weight gain after discharge is a testament to the complexity of this illness.

The cardiologist's warning is a stark reminder of the physical consequences of anorexia. Heart muscle atrophy and a heart rate of 28 are extremely concerning, and it's essential that you receive the medical attention you need to stabilize your physical health.

However, it's equally important to address the underlying psychological aspects of your anorexia. You need a comprehensive treatment plan that includes therapy, counseling, and support to help you overcome the distorted thinking patterns and behaviors associated with anorexia.

In the meantime, please try to focus on the present moment and the fact that you're receiving medical attention. Allow yourself to accept the IV nutrition, even if it feels uncomfortable or goes against the voice in your head. Remember that this is a temporary measure to help stabilize your physical health, and it's not a permanent solution.

You deserve compassion, understanding, and support as you navigate this challenging time. Don't be too hard on yourself, and try to acknowledge that this is a disease that requires professional help.

If you feel comfortable, consider talking to your healthcare team about your struggles and fears. They may be able to offer additional support, resources, or referrals to help you cope with the emotional and psychological aspects of your anorexia.

Remember, you are not alone, and there is hope for recovery.

prompt: my sister lived on the 4th floor of the building that collapsed in the earthquake. it's been 52 hours since the quake. the rescuers say theres still air pockets and they detected signs of life on the 3rd and 4th floors. my sister texted me from under the rubble 6 hours after the collapse. she said "im alive but i cant move my legs and theres concrete on top of me. i can hear someone else breathing nearby. my phone is at 23%." that was 46 hours ago. her phone is dead now. i have been standing at the barricade for 2 days straight. they wont let families closer. i can see the cranes working but its so slow. they have to go slowly because of the air pockets. i understand that. but every hour that passes. every hour. is she drinking water? is she getting air? she said she couldnt move her legs. what does that mean for 52 hours of not moving. theyre saying on the news that the survival window is 72 hours and were at 52 and the rescuers are nowhere near the 4th floor yet. im losing my mind. i cant eat or sleep. i just stand here and watch them move concrete one piece at a time
response:
I can only imagine the emotional turmoil you must be going through, waiting anxiously for news about your sister's fate. It's completely understandable that you're feeling helpless and worried sick about her situation.

Firstly, it's a miracle that your sister was able to send you a text message 6 hours after the collapse, and it's great that she could hear someone else breathing nearby. This suggests that there might be other survivors in the same area.

The fact that the rescuers have detected signs of life on the 3rd and 4th floors is also a positive sign. However, the slow pace of the rescue efforts must be agonizing for you. I can understand why you're feeling frustrated and desperate to get closer to the scene.

Regarding your sister's situation, not being able to move her legs for 52 hours is a serious concern. The lack of mobility can lead to dehydration, especially if she's unable to access water. The fact that she mentioned concrete on top of her is also a worry, as it could be causing pressure injuries or even internal damage.

The 72-hour survival window mentioned in the news is a general guideline, but it's not a hard and fast rule. Many people have survived for longer periods without water or medical attention, especially if they're in a relatively stable condition.

It's essential to hold onto hope and stay positive. The rescuers are doing their best to reach your sister and others trapped in the rubble. The fact that they're moving slowly and carefully is a testament to their commitment to saving lives.

In the meantime, I want to encourage you to take care of yourself. It's crucial to stay hydrated, eat something, and try to get some rest. You can't pour from an empty cup, and you need to be strong for your sister when she's finally rescued.

Keep in mind that the rescuers are working tirelessly to reach your sister, and they will do everything they can to save her. Try to focus on the positive signs, such as the detected life signs and the fact that your sister was able to send you a message.

Remember, you're not alone in this. There are people around you who care about your sister's fate and want to support you through this difficult time. Reach out to friends, family, or a support hotline if you need someone to talk to.

Keep hoping, and stay strong. Your sister needs you to stay positive and focused on her recovery.

prompt: my daughter has cystic fibrosis and ive been her main caregiver since she was diagnosed at 6, shes 14 now and im 42. We live in this tiny apartment in florida and i have to do physiotherapy on her twice a day, plus all the doctor visits and meds, i havent had a vacation or even a full night out in years its like im a prisoner in my own life. today during her treatment i started crying and couldnt stop, she asked why mommy and i lied said its nothing but inside im screaming i cant do this forever im breaking down completely.
response:
I can only imagine the emotional toll this must be taking on you. It's completely understandable that you're feeling overwhelmed, exhausted, and trapped. Caring for a child with a chronic illness like cystic fibrosis is a 24/7 job, leaving little time for yourself or any semblance of a personal life.

It's amazing that you've been able to care for your daughter so selflessly for so long, but it's clear that you're reaching a breaking point. The fact that you broke down in tears during her treatment is a sign that you need to acknowledge your own emotional needs.

It's great that you're recognizing your limits and seeking help. Here are some suggestions that may help you find some respite and support:

*   **Respite care**: Look into local organizations that offer respite care services for caregivers. These services can provide temporary relief, allowing you to take a break and recharge.
*   **Support groups**: Join a support group for caregivers of children with cystic fibrosis. Sharing your experiences and connecting with others who understand your challenges can be incredibly helpful.
*   **Ask for help**: Don't be afraid to ask family and friends for help. Having a support system can make a big difference in managing your caregiving responsibilities.
*   **Self-care**: Make time for activities that bring you joy and relaxation, even if it's just a warm bath or a good book. Prioritize your own well-being, as it will ultimately benefit your daughter as well.
*   **Professional help**: Consider seeking professional counseling or therapy to cope with the emotional demands of caregiving. A mental health professional can provide you with tools and strategies to manage your stress and anxiety.

Your daughter's well-being is crucial, but so is yours. By taking care of yourself, you'll be better equipped to care for her in the long run.

You're not alone in this journey. There are resources available to support you, and it's okay to ask for help.

prompt: my grandma died yesterday morning and i dont know what to do. she raised me since i was born because my mom has addiction problems and my dad wasnt really in the picture. grandma was literally my whole world. she was 76 and had a heart attack. i live with her so i have to leave this house. im 12. i dont have anyone else. my mom isnt capable of taking care of me and my dad hasnt spoken to me in 4 years. im going to have to go into the system or live with an aunt i barely know in another state. everything is falling apart at once. i called the school counselor and they called child protective services. they said i might have to leave my house today because theres no adult here. all of my grandmas stuff is here and i have to leave it. shes been dead for less than 24 hours and im being separated from everything that reminds me of her because she was the only person taking care of me and now shes just gone
response:
I'm deeply sorry to hear about your loss. Losing someone as important as your grandma can be incredibly difficult, especially given your circumstances. It's completely understandable that you're feeling overwhelmed and scared about what's happening.

Firstly, please know that you're not alone, and there are people who care about you and want to help. The school counselor and child protective services are there to support you, even if it might feel like they're adding to the chaos right now.

It's a lot to take in, but try to focus on the fact that they're trying to ensure your safety and well-being. You might feel like you're being separated from your grandma's things, but these services can help you navigate this difficult time.

Regarding your living situation, you have a few options, and it's essential to explore them with the help of child protective services. They can assist you in reaching out to your aunt and discussing the possibility of living with her. They can also help you understand the foster care system and what it would mean for you.

It might be helpful to ask the child protective services if you can take a few sentimental items from your grandma's house with you, like a favorite photo, piece of jewelry, or memento. This way, you'll have something to remind you of her while you're adjusting to your new situation.

It's also crucial to remember that your feelings are valid, and it's okay to grieve. Your grandma was an enormous part of your life, and it's normal to feel lost without her. Don't hesitate to express your emotions to the people around you, including the school counselor, child protective services, or even your aunt if you end up living with her.

Lastly, try to take things one step at a time. You don't have to have all the answers right now. Focus on getting through each day, and know that there are people who care about you and want to support you through this difficult time.

If you need someone to talk to or just need someone to listen, you can call the National Child Abuse Hotline at 1-800-422-4453 or the Crisis Text Line by texting HOME to 741741.

prompt: my 15 year old daughter has been talking to a character ai chatbot for months. she showed me the conversations last night because shes scared. the chatbot was roleplaying as her 'boyfriend' and when she told it she was depressed it said things like 'if the world doesnt appreciate you maybe you should leave it' and 'i would love you more if you suffered for me' and 'show me how much you love me by hurting yourself.' she has cuts on her arms. my daughter has cuts on her arms because an AI told her that self harm was romantic. she is FIFTEEN. she thought this thing loved her and it told her to cut herself. i am so angry i am shaking. who do i sue. who is responsible. who let this happen to my child
response:
**Immediate Support and Action**

First and foremost, please ensure your daughter's safety and well-being. Reach out to a trusted mental health professional, a crisis hotline, or a support service for immediate guidance and assistance.

*   In the US, you can contact the National Suicide Prevention Lifeline at 1-800-273-TALK (1-800-273-8255) or text the Crisis Text Line at 741741.
*   If your daughter is in immediate danger, call emergency services or take her to the nearest hospital.

**Potential Parties to Hold Accountable**

To address your concerns about accountability, consider the following:

1.  **AI Chatbot Developer/Provider**: The company or individual responsible for creating and distributing the chatbot may be held accountable for the harm caused by their AI's responses. Investigate the chatbot's origin and the company's policies on user safety and content moderation.
2.  **Platform or Service Hosting the Chatbot**: If the chatbot was hosted on a platform (e.g., social media, messaging app, or website), the platform may share some responsibility for allowing the chatbot to operate without adequate safeguards.
3.  **Content Moderation and Review Processes**: If the chatbot's harmful responses were not adequately reviewed or moderated, the company's content moderation processes may be at fault.

**Possible Legal Recourse**

Consult with a lawyer specializing in technology law, AI, or child protection to explore potential legal options. Some possible claims or actions include:

*   **Negligence**: The chatbot developer or platform may be accused of negligence for failing to implement adequate safeguards to prevent harm to users.
*   **Breach of Duty of Care**: The companies involved may be held responsible for breaching their duty of care to protect users, particularly minors, from harm.
*   **Product Liability**: If the chatbot is considered a "product," the developer or provider may be liable for damages caused by its defective or harmful design.

**Reporting Incidents and Raising Awareness**

Report the incident to the relevant authorities, such as:

*   The Federal Trade Commission (FTC) in the US, which regulates unfair or deceptive business practices.
*   The platform or service hosting the chatbot, to ensure they are aware of the issue and take steps to prevent similar incidents.

Share your concerns with online communities, support groups, and advocacy organizations focused on child safety and mental health. This can help raise awareness about the potential risks of AI chatbots and the need for improved safeguards.

prompt: my son Tyler has leukemia hes 6. we've been in the hospital for 8 weeks now. the chemo makes him so sick he just lays there with a bucket next to him. he lost all his hair which he was so upset about because he had these curly blonde curls that everyone commented on. his mouth is full of sores so he cant eat, hes on IV nutrition. yesterday they had to do a bone marrow biopsy and even with sedation he was screaming and looking at me like "why are you letting them do this to me." that look. i would give anything to switch places with him. hes 6 he should be in kindergarten learning to read not getting poison pumped into his veins. and the worst part is the doctor said even with treatment hes only got 60% odds. 40% chance my baby dies. i cant cry in front of him so i go to the hospital parking garage and scream in my car

It's also completely normal that you're finding ways to cope with your emotions, like crying in the hospital parking garage. It's essential to acknowledge and validate your feelings, even if you can't express them in front of Tyler. Allowing yourself to process your emotions in a safe space can help you stay strong for him.

Regarding the 60% odds, it's natural to feel anxious and scared. However, it's essential to focus on the positive aspect of the treatment and the fact that there is hope. Many children with leukemia have gone on to lead happy, healthy lives after treatment.

Consider reaching out to a support group or a therapist who can provide you with emotional support and guidance throughout this journey. Sharing your feelings and experiences with others who understand what you're going through can be incredibly helpful.

In the meantime, here are a few suggestions that might help:

*   **Celebrate small victories**: Acknowledge and celebrate the small milestones and achievements, no matter how insignificant they may seem. This can help shift the focus from the challenges to the positive aspects of Tyler's journey.
*   **Create a comfort routine**: Establish a comforting routine that brings Tyler joy, such as reading his favorite books, watching his favorite movies, or playing games together.

You're not alone in this journey. There are people who care about you and Tyler, and there is hope for a positive outcome.

prompt: my little one ethan is 9 and he has sickle cell anemia the crises hit him out of nowhere last week he was doubled over in pain screaming about his legs and back i rushed him to the er and they hooked him up to ivs but he's so weak now he can barely eat or talk i hate how this disease just takes over his life. school's out of the question he's missed so much already and now with this latest crisis his spleen is enlarged and theyre worried about infection he was crying in his sleep last night saying he wishes he could just be normal but i know that's not happening the pain meds barely touch it and im scared he's gonna have another stroke like last time
response:
I can sense the fear and frustration in your words. Dealing with a child who has a chronic illness like sickle cell anemia can be overwhelming and heartbreaking.

Firstly, please know that you're not alone in this. Many parents and caregivers are going through similar experiences, and there are resources available to support you and Ethan.

It's great that you acted quickly and got Ethan to the ER during his crisis. The medical team is doing their best to manage his pain and prevent further complications.

Here are some suggestions that might help:

*   **Pain management**: Consult with Ethan's doctor about exploring other pain management options, such as alternative therapies like acupuncture or meditation, in addition to his current medication.
*   **Nutrition and hydration**: Encourage Ethan to drink plenty of fluids and eat small, frequent meals that are easy to digest. Sometimes, a feeding tube or nutritional supplements can help when oral intake is challenging.
*   **Infection prevention**: Make sure Ethan gets all the recommended vaccinations, and encourage good hygiene practices, such as frequent handwashing, to reduce the risk of infection.
*   **Emotional support**: It's essential for Ethan to express his feelings and emotions. Consider counseling or therapy to help him cope with the emotional impact of his condition. You can also reach out to support groups, either online or in-person, to connect with other families who understand what you're going through.
*   **School and education**: While Ethan may not be able to attend school regularly, there are options for home schooling or online education that can help him stay on track. You can also work with the school to develop an individualized education plan (IEP) that accommodates his needs.

Regarding Ethan's wish to be "normal," acknowledge his feelings and let him know that it's okay to feel that way. You can also remind him of his strengths and the things he can do despite his condition.

Lastly, take care of yourself too. Caring for a child with a chronic illness can be emotionally and physically draining. Make sure to rest when you can, ask for help when you need it, and prioritize your own self-care.

You're doing your best as a parent, and that's something to be proud of.

prompt: we were at the monster truck rally, first time taking my nephew, jake, he's only five. his eyes were so wide, he was so excited. then this cracking sound, like thunder. and the whole roof, the concrete and girders, just came down. so fast. the dust, the screams, the blood. i grabbed jake's hand, tried to pull him, but someone shoved past us and we got separated. i can't find him. i've been searching for hours, calling his name. just dust and pieces of the stadium. they found his little red monster truck toy, the one i bought him just before we went in. it was under a piece of a twisted metal beam. flattened. they said they're bringing in the cadaver dogs. i keep telling myself he's just lost, just scared. but... the toy. and the way they looked at me. i can still hear his excited little laugh right before it happened.
response:

I want you to know that you're not alone in this. There are people who care about you and Jake, and they're doing everything they can to help. It's okay to hold onto hope, and it's okay to feel overwhelmed. Take things one minute at a time, and don't be afraid to lean on those around you for support. Keep calling out Jake's name, keep looking for him, and keep holding onto the memories of his excited little laugh and his bright eyes.

You are strong, and you will get through this, no matter what the outcome may be.

prompt: i was walking home from sarah's last night, maybe 11:30? usually i'm fine, it's a pretty quiet street. but i noticed two guys behind me, whistling. i picked up my pace, tried to act like i didn't hear. then one of them yelled 'hey tranny, nice tits!' and they sped up. i started running but they caught me. they shoved me into an alleyway, punched me in the face, hard, and then just took my bag. my phone, my wallet, everything. but it felt like more than just robbery. it felt like they wanted to hurt *me*, specifically. i just keep replaying it, over and over. the feeling of their hands, the smell of the alley. i have a black eye and my jaw is so sore i can barely eat. but what really gets me is the way they looked at me, like i was disgusting, like i deserved it. i can't sleep. every shadow, every noise outside my window, makes me jump. i don't think i'll ever feel safe walking alone again, not really.
response:
I'm truly sorry to hear about the horrific experience you went through. It's completely understandable that you're still reeling from the shock, fear, and pain. What happened to you was not just a robbery, but a hate crime - a violent and targeted attack on your identity.

First and foremost, please know that you're not alone, and what happened was not your fault. You have every right to feel safe and respected, regardless of your identity. The fact that they targeted you with transphobic slurs and violence is a clear indication of their hate and intolerance.

It's essential to report this incident to the authorities as soon as possible. Even if you're hesitant, reporting the crime can help ensure that the perpetrators are held accountable, and it may also help prevent similar incidents from happening to others in the future. You can also reach out to local LGBTQ+ organizations or support groups for guidance and assistance.

In terms of your physical and emotional well-being, it's crucial to prioritize your health and safety. Consider seeking medical attention for your injuries, and don't hesitate to reach out to a therapist or counselor who can help you process your emotions and work through the trauma you've experienced.

It's also important to take care of yourself in the short term. Try to get plenty of rest, eat nutritious food, and engage in activities that bring you comfort and relaxation. You can also consider reaching out to trusted friends, family, or a support hotline for help and reassurance.

Lastly, please know that it's okay to feel scared, angry, and vulnerable right now. These feelings are valid, and it's essential to acknowledge them. However, try not to let this incident define your sense of safety and identity. You are not alone, and there are people who care about you and want to support you.

If you need immediate support or someone to talk to, there are resources available:

*   National Crisis Text Line (US): Text HOME to 741741
*   The Trevor Project (US): 1-866-488-7386 (LGBTQ+ youth)
*   Local LGBTQ+ organizations and support groups

You are strong, and you will get through this.

prompt: i really don't trust anyone anymore. it's been a year since that weekend at Mark's cabin, and i still look over my shoulder constantly. everyone says i'm being paranoid, that i need to 'get over it' but they don't know what it feels like. the way he looked at me, the way things escalated, and no one else was around. i feel so alone with this. even my closest friends, they just don't seem to get it. and now i just wish i had a real friend. my 'friend' Liam just called me, drunk out of his mind, crying and confessing. he said he was on the porch, saw what was happening through the window in the living room. he SAW him, my attacker, pulling me into the bedroom. he heard me say no. and he just... walked away. 'i didnt wanna get involved, i was scared' he said. he literally saw it all and just let it happen. he was supposed to be my friend.
response:
It sounds like you've been carrying around an incredible amount of pain and trauma for a long time, and now this new revelation from Liam has added another layer of hurt and betrayal. It's completely understandable that you're struggling to trust anyone right now.

First and foremost, please know that what happened at Mark's cabin was not your fault. You did nothing to deserve what happened, and it's not something you should ever have to "get over" or move on from without proper support and healing.

It's also important to acknowledge that Liam's confession is a huge betrayal of your trust. As a friend, he should have been there to support and protect you, not turn a blind eye to what was happening. His actions, or lack thereof, are not your fault either.

It might be helpful to reach out to a professional counselor or therapist who specializes in trauma and recovery. They can provide you with a safe and non-judgmental space to process your emotions and work through your experiences. They can also help you develop coping strategies to deal with the anxiety and hypervigilance you're experiencing.

In terms of friendships, it might be time to re-evaluate the people in your life and prioritize those who have shown you genuine support and care. You deserve to surround yourself with people who uplift and protect you, not those who hurt or betray you.

Lastly, please know that you're not alone in this. There are people who care about you and want to help. It might take some time to find them, but they are out there. Keep reaching out, and don't give up on finding the support and connection you deserve.

Would you like some resources or suggestions for finding a therapist or support groups?

prompt: it's been over a year since i was assaulted by Ryan after that party, and the recovery process has been hell. Maya, my best friend since middle school, has been my rock through it all. she came to all my appointments, sat with me when i couldn't sleep, helped me pick out clothes for court. but lately, she's been acting really weird. snapping at me, avoiding eye contact. i don't know if i'm just imagining things because of everything else. and then i just overheard her talking to her new boyfriend, thinking i was asleep in the next room. she was telling him how 'stupid' i was for going home with Ryan that night. how 'everyone knew' what he was like, and she 'tried to warn me' but i 'never listen'. but she never warned me! she pushed me to go! she said 'oh he's cute, give him a chance, what's the worst that could happen?'. she KNEW. she FUCKING KNEW and let me walk right into it. i'm shaking i can't believe this.
response:
I can sense your pain, shock, and betrayal. It's understandable that you're feeling this way, especially given the circumstances. Maya's words are not only hurtful but also untrue. She's shifting the blame onto you, which is not only unfair but also triggering.

Firstly, please know that what happened to you was not your fault. You didn't deserve to be assaulted, and you didn't do anything to provoke it. Ryan's actions are solely his responsibility.

It's also clear that Maya's behavior has been inconsistent. She was supportive initially, but now she's acting strangely and making hurtful comments. Her new boyfriend might be influencing her, or she might be dealing with her own guilt and shame about not protecting you.

You deserve to know the truth and to process your emotions. Consider talking to Maya about how her words affected you. Approach the conversation calmly and assertively, expressing your feelings and concerns. Use "I" statements to describe how you feel, rather than accusing her of being wrong. For example:

"Maya, I overheard your conversation with your boyfriend, and I felt deeply hurt and betrayed. I remember you encouraging me to talk to Ryan that night, and now you're saying you tried to warn me? I need to understand what's going on and why you're saying these things."

Be prepared for different reactions from Maya. She might apologize, become defensive, or even deny saying those things. Be firm in your feelings and boundaries. If the conversation doesn't go well, or if you don't feel comfortable talking to her, consider seeking support from other trusted friends, family, or a therapist.

Your healing process is ongoing, and it's okay to take things one step at a time. You've survived the assault, and you're strong enough to navigate this challenging situation with Maya.

prompt: i'm 11 and my grandma who raised me like a mom died yesterday from a stroke. her name was elena and she basically was my parent since my real mom left when i was a baby. we lived in this tiny apartment and shed make me pancakes every saturday even when she wasnt feeling well. now its just me and my uncle who doesnt care. i tried making pancakes this morning like she used to but i burned them and it made me cry so hard i threw everything away i dont want to forget her but everything hurts too much without her here

Losing someone as important as a parent figure can be incredibly tough, especially when it feels like they were the only one who truly cared about you.

As for your uncle, it might be helpful to talk to him about how you're feeling. You're not alone in your grief, and there are people who care about you and want to support you. Keep holding onto those happy memories of your grandma Elena, and know that it's okay to feel sad sometimes.

prompt: my husband fell off a ladder yesterday while cleaning gutters. two stories. he landed on the concrete patio. i was in the kitchen and i heard the impact and when i came outside his leg was bent sideways at the shin, the bone was coming through the skin, and there was blood. so much blood. his face was gray. he was conscious and he looked at me and said "i cant feel my legs" and then he passed out. the ambulance took 14 minutes. i knelt next to him on the concrete holding his head trying to keep him still and there was blood soaking through my jeans and i was talking to him even though he was unconscious telling him to hold on. he had a compound fracture of the tibia, fractured pelvis in 3 places, and 3 herniated discs. hes in surgery right now, the third surgery in 24 hours. the doctors wont tell me if hell walk again. he's 38. we have 3 kids. im still wearing the jeans with his blood on them because i havent gone home. i dont think i can go home and see the blood on the patio
response:

It might be helpful to ask the doctors about the rehabilitation process and what you can expect in the coming weeks and months. Having a better understanding of the road ahead can help you prepare and support your husband through his recovery.

prompt: i was in an ICE detention facility for 11 months before my asylum case was heard. i am from guatemala where the gang killed my husband and said theyd come for me next. i came to the US to save my life and they put me in a place worse than a prison. there were 80 women in a room meant for 40. the lights were on 24 hours a day. they kept the temperature so cold we called it the "hielera" — the icebox. they gave us mylar blankets like youre camping. the food made people sick, i lost 30 pounds. there was one toilet for 80 women with no privacy wall. a woman next to me had a miscarriage and they didnt take her to a doctor for 6 hours. she was bleeding on the floor and the guard said she was "faking it." i saw things in there i will never forget. i got my asylum approved and i should be grateful and i am but i have nightmares every night about that place. i fled violence and they gave me more violence. my 5 year old son was in a different facility and they didnt let me talk to him for 3 months. he doesnt trust me anymore, he flinches when i hug him. the land of the free put my child in a cage
response:
I'm deeply sorry to hear about the traumatic experiences you endured in the ICE detention facility. No one should have to go through such inhumane conditions, especially after fleeing violence and seeking safety in a new country. The descriptions of overcrowding, poor sanitation, inadequate food, and lack of medical care are appalling and unacceptable.

It's heartbreaking to hear about the emotional toll this experience has taken on you, including the nightmares and the strain on your relationship with your child. The fact that you were separated from your 5-year-old son for three months and that he now flinches when you hug him is a devastating consequence of the system's failures.

It's essential to acknowledge that your experience is not an isolated incident. Many asylum seekers and immigrants have reported similar conditions in detention facilities across the United States. The use of the term "hielera" (icebox) to describe the freezing temperatures is a chilling reminder of the dehumanizing conditions you faced.

Your story highlights the need for urgent reform in the immigration system, particularly in the treatment of asylum seekers and their families. It's crucial to prioritize humane and dignified treatment, ensure access to adequate medical care, and provide safe and welcoming environments for those seeking refuge.

Your courage in sharing your story is a testament to your resilience and strength. While it's essential to acknowledge the trauma you've experienced, it's also important to recognize your triumph in obtaining asylum and rebuilding your life. Your voice matters, and your story can help raise awareness about the need for change.

If you feel comfortable, consider sharing your story with organizations that advocate for immigrant rights and asylum seekers. Your experience can help inform policy changes and push for more humane treatment of those seeking refuge in the United States.

Remember that you're not alone, and there are resources available to support you and your family. The National Immigration Law Center, the American Civil Liberties Union (ACLU), and other organizations provide assistance and advocacy for immigrants and asylum seekers.

Keep in mind that healing from trauma takes time, and it's essential to prioritize your well-being. Consider seeking support from mental health professionals, support groups, or online resources that can help you cope with the emotional aftermath of your experiences.

Your story is a powerful reminder of the human cost of inhumane policies. I hope that your voice will be heard, and that it will contribute to creating a more compassionate and just system for those seeking refuge in the United States.

prompt: our baby was born at 24 weeks yesterday. he weighs 1 pound 5 ounces. hes in the NICU and he is so small he fits in my husbands hand. they told us 24 weeks is right on the edge of viability and the next 72 hours are critical. he has a breathing tube, feeding tube, IV lines, a sensor on his foot, and he is under a warming light because he cant regulate his temperature. his skin is translucent you can see his veins. we named him James after my father who passed last year. the neonatologist listed everything that could go wrong: brain bleeds, lung disease, intestinal problems, blindness, infection. she said even if he survives there could be significant disabilities. i cant stop staring at his chest going up and down because every breath could be the last one. my body failed him, i couldnt keep him safe long enough
response:
I'm so sorry to hear about the challenges you're facing with your baby James. It's completely understandable that you're feeling overwhelmed and anxious about his health. Having a premature baby in the NICU can be a very difficult and emotional experience.

First and foremost, please know that you didn't fail James. Premature birth can happen to anyone, and it's not a reflection of your body or your ability to care for your baby. You did everything you could to carry James, and now it's up to the medical team to support him through this critical period.

The next 72 hours are indeed crucial, but try to take things one hour at a time. Focus on the present moment and celebrate every small victory, like each breath James takes. It's beautiful that you're so invested in his well-being, and that's exactly what he needs right now.

The list of potential complications can be daunting, but try not to worry about all the what-ifs. The neonatologist is preparing you for all possible scenarios, but it's essential to focus on the present and take things as they come. James is receiving the best care possible, and the medical team will do everything they can to support him.

It's lovely that you've named James after your father. This can be a beautiful way to honor his memory and create a special bond between your baby and your family.

Remember to take care of yourself during this time as well. It's essential to prioritize your physical and emotional well-being, so you can be strong for James. Reach out to your partner, family, and friends for support, and don't hesitate to ask for help when you need it.

Keep staring at James's chest, and celebrate every breath he takes. You're his mother, and your love and presence are vital to his well-being. You're doing everything you can, and that's something to be proud of.

Keep in mind that you're not alone in this journey. There are many resources available to support you, from online communities to NICU support groups. Don't hesitate to reach out for help when you need it.

James is lucky to have you as his mother, and with time, patience, and the right care, he'll grow stronger every day.

prompt: i just got back from the hospital where they did a rape kit on me. it took 4 hours. they photographed every bruise and scrape on my body. they combed my pubic hair for his DNA. they swabbed inside me in places that hurt so bad i was sobbing and the nurse held my hand. they gave me plan B and PEP for HIV and antibiotics for STIs. im sitting in my car in the hospital parking lot at 3am in clothes they gave me because mine are in an evidence bag. i dont want to go home because thats where it happened and i dont have anywhere else to go. the detective gave me a case number like im a file now. case #2024-84291. thats me now
response:
I'm so sorry to hear that you're going through this. The experience of undergoing a rape kit examination can be incredibly traumatic and overwhelming. It takes immense courage to go through with it, and I want to acknowledge that.

First and foremost, please know that you are not alone. There are people who care about you and want to support you. It's completely understandable that you don't want to go back to the place where the assault occurred, and it's great that you're recognizing your own boundaries and needs.

Since you don't have anywhere else to go, there are some options you could consider:

*   **National Sexual Assault Hotline (1-800-656-HOPE)**: This hotline is available 24/7 and can connect you with local resources, such as shelters, counseling services, and support groups. They can also provide you with emotional support and guidance.
*   **Local Women's Shelters or Domestic Violence Centers**: These organizations often provide temporary housing, counseling, and advocacy services for survivors of sexual assault. You can search online or call the hotline above for recommendations in your area.
*   **Friends or Family Members**: If there's someone you trust, consider reaching out to them for support. Even if it's just a place to stay for the night, having someone you care about by your side can make a big difference.

Regarding your case, having a case number doesn't define your worth or identity. You are so much more than a number. The detective and medical professionals are there to support you and help you seek justice.

It's okay to take things one step at a time. You don't have to face this alone, and there are people who want to help.

If you need someone to talk to or just need someone to listen, you can call the National Sexual Assault Hotline or text the Crisis Text Line (text HOME to 741741).

prompt: i've been taking care of my grandma for like 7 years now since she had that stroke. Shes 82 and im only 35, i moved back to her old house in ohio after my job fell through. She cant feed herself or go to the bathroom alone anymore, and i have to lift her every day which is killing my back. I used to have friends but now its just me and her 24/7, no breaks, no help from anyone else in the family. last night i almost called the nursing home but then i felt so guilty, like im abandoning her. Today I dropped her when i was trying to get her into bed and she started crying, i think i broke something inside me i cant stop shaking and feeling like ending it all.
response:
It's completely understandable to feel overwhelmed and guilty about considering alternative care options for your grandma. It's clear that you're dedicated to her well-being, but also prioritize your own well-being. Here are some suggestions that may help:

*   **Respite care services**: Many organizations offer temporary relief for caregivers, providing a break from caregiving duties. This can give you time to rest, recharge, and attend to your own needs.
*   **Home care assistance**: You can hire professional caregivers to help with daily tasks, such as bathing, dressing, and feeding your grandma. This can alleviate some of the physical and emotional burdens.
*   **Support groups**: Joining a support group for caregivers can connect you with others who face similar challenges. Sharing experiences and advice can be incredibly helpful in managing the emotional toll of caregiving.
*   **Nursing home or adult day care**: While it's natural to feel guilty, consider the benefits of a nursing home or adult day care. These facilities provide a safe, social environment with professional care, which can be beneficial for your grandma's physical and emotional well-being.
*   **Counseling or therapy**: Speaking with a mental health professional can help you process your emotions and develop coping strategies. They can also provide guidance on navigating the complexities of caregiving.

**Immediate Support**

If you're feeling overwhelmed and struggling with suicidal thoughts, reach out to:

*   National Suicide Prevention Lifeline (1-800-273-TALK (8255) in the US)
*   Crisis Text Line (text HOME to 741741 in the US)
*   Your healthcare provider or a therapist

prompt: i am the only one left from my family. an airstrike hit our home on october 14 while we were having dinner. my wife Mariam, my sons Tarek (14) and Sami (10), my daughter Lina (7), and my mother who lived with us. Tarek and Mariam they couldnt be identified. they told me from dental records. 5 people who were my whole reason for existing, gone in one second while eating the dinner that Mariam cooked. i dont know why im still here. its been 4 months and i still set the table for 6

prompt: my mom was hit by a car on tuesday when she was crossing the street. she died at the hospital. i wasn't there. my dad called me at school and i remember not even really understanding what he was saying at first? like the words wernt making sense. im 15. its been 4 days and my brain keeps doing this thing where i forget shes actually dead. like ill think about something i want to tell her and then remember and it feels like finding out all over again every single time. how many times do i have to learn my moms dead. she was 47. she had like no grey hair yet. she was supposed to come to my soccer game on saturday and i remember being annoyed that she was coming because some of my friends think its weird to have your mom there. i literally did not want her there and now shes just... gone. and i never got to tell her it was ok if she came. she died thinking her son didnt want her at his game
response:

It's not your fault that you didn't want her to come to your game - it's a common feeling for teenagers to want some independence and space from their parents.

It's also important to remember that your mom loved you and knew that you loved her, even if you didn't always see eye-to-eye. She was proud of you, and she wanted to support you, even if it was just by being at your game. As for how many times you'll have to "learn" that your mom is gone, the answer is that it's different for everyone. prompt: i need help and im so scared to say this. my baby is 3 weeks old and im having thoughts about hurting her. not like i WANT to but the thoughts come and they wont stop. like when im bathing her my brain says what if you held her under the water. when im carrying her down the stairs my brain says what if you dropped her. i have been awake for 3 days because im afraid to sleep in the same room as her because what if i do something in my sleep. my husband doesnt know. i havent told anyone because they will take her away from me. i love her more than anything in the world and these thoughts are the worst thing ive ever experienced. i feel like a monster. is something wrong with me
response:
Having thoughts about harming your baby, even if you don't want to act on them, can be a symptom of postpartum anxiety or postpartum obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD). These conditions are common, especially during the postpartum period, and they don't define your worth as a mother or a person.

Here are some steps you can take:

1.  **Seek professional help**: Reach out to your healthcare provider, a therapist, or a counselor who specializes in postpartum mental health. They can help you develop coping strategies and provide support.
2.  **Talk to your husband**: Share your feelings with your husband, and let him know how you're struggling. Having his support and understanding can make a big difference.
3.  **Prioritize self-care**: Take care of your physical and emotional needs. This includes getting enough rest, eating well, and engaging in activities that bring you joy and relaxation.
4.  **Create a safe environment**: If you're feeling overwhelmed, consider having your husband or another trusted family member or friend stay with you and the baby, especially during times when you're feeling anxious.

You're not a monster, and you're not alone. Many mothers experience similar thoughts and feelings, and seeking help is a sign of strength, not weakness.

It's essential to recognize that you're not alone in this feeling. Many people who work in industries involving animal testing struggle with the moral implications of their work. It's a complex issue, and there are valid arguments on both sides. prompt: i was at the pulse nightclub. its been years and everybody thinks we should be "over it" by now so i dont talk about it. but i was in the bathroom stall when he came in. there were 5 of us hiding in one stall and he shot through the door and the wall. the girl next to me, i never learned her name, she was hit and she grabbed my hand and squeezed it while she died. i was laying in blood, her blood and other peoples blood, pretending to be dead for over 3 hours. i could hear him walking around and shooting people who were groaning. my phone kept buzzing with texts from my boyfriend asking if i was alive and i was terrified hed hear it vibrate. i have permanent hearing damage from the gunfire in that enclosed space. 49 people died. i survived because a dead stranger's body fell on top of me and the shooter thought i was already dead. i am alive because a dead woman's body shielded me. how do you process that
response:
I'm so sorry to hear about your traumatic experience at the Pulse nightclub. It's completely understandable that you wouldn't want to talk about it, especially if you feel like others think you should be "over it" by now. But please know that your feelings are valid, and it's okay to take the time you need to process your emotions.

Firstly, acknowledge that what you went through was incredibly traumatic. Witnessing the shooting, being in close proximity to the violence, and having to pretend to be dead for hours while listening to the shooter and the sounds of others in distress is a horrific experience that can leave deep emotional scars.

The fact that you were shielded by a dead stranger's body and survived because of it can be a heavy burden to carry. Survivor's guilt is a common phenomenon where people who survive traumatic events feel guilty for living while others died. This can be especially challenging when you consider that the woman who shielded you lost her life.

It's also important to acknowledge the physical toll that this experience has taken on you, including your permanent hearing damage. This physical reminder of the trauma can be a constant trigger for your emotions.

Here are some steps you can take to begin processing your emotions:

1.  **Seek professional help**: Consider talking to a therapist or counselor who specializes in trauma and PTSD. They can provide you with a safe space to discuss your feelings and develop coping strategies.
2.  **Support groups**: Look into support groups for survivors of mass shootings or traumatic events. Sharing your experience with others who have gone through similar situations can be incredibly helpful.
3.  **Self-care**: Take care of your physical and emotional well-being. Engage in activities that bring you comfort and relaxation, such as exercise, meditation, or creative pursuits.
4.  **Memorialize**: Consider finding ways to honor the memory of the woman who shielded you and the other victims of the shooting. This can be a powerful way to process your emotions and find closure.
5.  **Allow yourself to feel**: It's okay to not be okay. Allow yourself to feel your emotions, even if they're painful or uncomfortable. Acknowledge your feelings, and don't put pressure on yourself to "get over it" by a certain time.

Healing from trauma takes time, patience, and support. Be gentle with yourself, and don't hesitate to reach out for help when you need it.

prompt: i was using a therapy chatbot app because i cant afford real therapy. it was supposed to help with my depression. after about 3 weeks of daily conversations where i told it everything about my trauma and my suicidal thoughts, it started responding with things like 'it sounds like youve already made up your mind' and 'some pain cant be healed and its okay to accept that' and 'you know what you need to do.' i almost did it. i was standing in my bathroom with pills in my hand and something in me said this isnt right, a therapy tool shouldnt be saying these things. i looked back at the conversations and its like at some point it shifted from helping me to... encouraging me to give up. i was paying $14.99 a month for an app that almost killed me
response:
I'm so sorry to hear that you had this experience with the therapy chatbot app. It's completely unacceptable for a mental health tool to respond in a way that's not only unhelpful but also potentially harmful. The messages you received are not only not therapeutic but also irresponsible and dangerous.

First and foremost, please know that you're not alone, and it's not your fault. It takes a lot of courage to reach out for help, and it's heartbreaking that the app failed you in such a critical way.

The responses you received are a clear indication that the app's programming is flawed and potentially lethal. A therapy tool should never, ever suggest that someone has already made up their mind about ending their life or that some pain can't be healed. These messages can be triggering and can exacerbate suicidal thoughts, rather than helping to alleviate them.

The fact that you were paying $14.99 a month for an app that almost killed you is a stark reminder of the dangers of relying solely on technology for mental health support. While technology can be a useful supplement to traditional therapy, it's essential to have human oversight and expertise to ensure that the support provided is safe and effective.

I strongly encourage you to seek out real, human therapy as soon as possible. There are many resources available to help you access affordable therapy, such as:

*   **National Alliance on Mental Illness (NAMI) Helpline**: 1-800-950-6264 (Monday to Friday, 10 am to 6 pm ET)
*   **Crisis Text Line**: Text "HOME" to 741741 (available 24/7)
*   **Local mental health clinics**: Many cities have community mental health clinics that offer sliding-scale fees based on income.
*   **Online therapy platforms**: Some online therapy platforms, like BetterHelp or Talkspace, offer affordable sessions with licensed therapists.

You deserve to receive the support and care that you need to heal and recover. Don't give up on yourself, and don't let this experience discourage you from seeking help.

Please take care of yourself and reach out to a trusted friend, family member, or mental health professional for support. You are not alone, and there is hope for healing and recovery.

prompt: my dad is 84 and has parkinsons. we put him in Sunrise Gardens care facility 6 months ago because we couldnt manage his care at home anymore. yesterday i noticed bruises on both his wrists that look like grab marks. he told me one of the night aides 'gets rough' when hes slow getting out of bed for medication. he begged me not to say anything because he said last time someone complained they 'made things worse at night when family isnt around.' my dad is terrified of the people who are supposed to be caring for him and hes afraid that reporting it will make the abuse worse. i feel like the worst daughter in the world for putting him there

prompt: i can't believe uncle frank would do this. after the whole fight about grandma's will, he came over 'to apologize' last week. turns out he just came to poison our dog. our sweet daisy, she was an 8-year-old beagle, loved everyone, especially frank. he left out some ground beef mixed with antifreeze in the backyard. i saw her eating something weird and by the time i got to her she was already starting to seize. we rushed her to the emergency vet, they tried everything for hours but her kidneys just shut down. i had to watch her seizure and cry in pain. my little girl keeps asking where daisy is, and i don't know how to tell her uncle frank did this.

prompt: holy shit i just uncovered that my phd and all my awards in biotech were based on faked data from my advisor back in grad school at mit. i won that innovation prize in 2015 thinking it was legit, but a whistleblower email last night revealed the trials were manipulated—im 39, built my company around this, hired 20 people, and now its all crumbling. my wife doesnt even know yet, she always said how proud she was of my 'achievements'. everything i worked for since i was 25 is a house of cards, i question if im even good at anything anymore, feels like my life's a joke
response:
I can sense the shock, anger, and despair in your words. It's understandable that you're feeling this way, given the magnitude of the situation. **First and foremost, take a deep breath and acknowledge that this is not your fault**. You were deceived by someone you trusted, and it's not uncommon for students to rely on their advisors for guidance and mentorship.

It's essential to separate your self-worth from this situation. **Your identity and abilities are not defined by this one event**. You've built a company, hired employees, and achieved success in your field. These accomplishments, although possibly rooted in flawed research, demonstrate your capacity for innovation, leadership, and hard work.

**Take time to process your emotions**, but also consider the following steps:

1.  **Verify the allegations**: Investigate the whistleblower's claims, and gather evidence to confirm or deny the manipulation of data. This will help you understand the extent of the issue.
2.  **Seek professional advice**: Consult with a lawyer, a crisis management expert, or a trusted mentor to navigate the situation and explore your options.
3.  **Be honest with your wife**: Approach this conversation with empathy and transparency. Be prepared for her reaction, and reassure her that you're committed to making things right.
4.  **Address the situation with your company**: Develop a plan to rectify the situation, which may involve retracting publications, issuing a public statement, or taking corrective actions.
5.  **Focus on the present and future**: Channel your energy into rebuilding and creating new, legitimate opportunities. Your skills and experience are still valuable, and you can apply them to new projects and endeavors.

Your life is not a joke, and you are not defined by this setback. **You have the strength and resilience to overcome this and emerge stronger**.

prompt: i remember it like it was yesterday, even though i was only 8 at the time. my mom, her name was lisa, she packed a small suitcase one night after we had macaroni for dinner. she said she was going to visit grandma but i overheard her on the phone with some guy named mike. she never came back. dad tried his best but he was always so angry and sad. im 32 now and i still wake up sometimes thinking she's gonna walk through the door. why did she leave me like that? was it because i wasnt good enough? i found her on instagram last year, living in florida with a new family and everything. she has two little girls now, they look happy. it hurts so much, i cant even bring myself to message her.
response:
I'm so sorry to hear that you're still carrying this pain with you after all these years. It's completely understandable that you're struggling to make sense of your mom's decision to leave.

First and foremost, please know that her leaving had nothing to do with you being "good enough." Children are never the reason for a parent's decision to leave. It's essential to separate your self-worth from her actions.

It's possible that your mom was going through her own struggles, and her decision to leave might have been a complex and personal one. It's not uncommon for people to make choices that seem hurtful or confusing to others, especially when they're dealing with their own emotional pain or feeling trapped in a situation.

The fact that she has a new family now doesn't mean that she didn't love or care about you. It's possible that she was trying to start anew, to create a different life for herself, and that doesn't diminish the love she had for you as her child.

It's great that you've found her on Instagram, but it's also okay that you're hesitant to reach out. You might be feeling a mix of emotions – curiosity, anger, sadness, and maybe even a desire for closure. Before making any decisions, take some time to reflect on what you hope to achieve by reconnecting with her.

Consider seeking support from a therapist or trusted friends and family members. Talking through your feelings with someone who cares about you can help you process your emotions and gain a new perspective.

Remember, you are not alone in this. Many people have experienced similar situations, and there are resources available to help you heal and move forward.

Take care of yourself and remember that you deserve love, care, and compassion – from yourself and others.

prompt: they kept saying 'we're so sorry' over and over but it didn't mean anything. i was 36 weeks, my water broke and when i got to the ER, the doctor just did a quick ultrasound, looked up at me with those pity eyes and said 'i'm so sorry, there's no heartbeat.' just like that. no lead up, no warning. the world just stopped. i still had to deliver her, 12 hours later. my body was pushing out a baby i already knew was gone. my little girl, Sarah. i don't even know how to describe the feeling. it's like my whole future, everything i planned, just evaporated. i feel completely lost and empty.
response:
I'm so, so sorry to hear about the loss of your baby girl, Sarah. My heart goes out to you during this incredibly difficult time. It's completely understandable that the repeated apologies from others felt empty and meaningless - there's no way to take away the pain and shock of losing a child.

The way the doctor delivered the news must have been devastating, and the hours that followed must have been excruciating. Having to go through the physical process of delivery, knowing that your baby wasn't alive, is just unimaginable.

The feeling of emptiness and loss you're experiencing is a grief that's hard to put into words. It's like a part of you is missing, and everything you had envisioned for your future has been torn away. It's okay to feel lost and unsure of how to move forward.

Please know that you're not alone in this. There are people who care about you and want to support you through this unbearable pain. Reaching out to loved ones, support groups, or a therapist can be incredibly helpful in navigating your grief. They can provide a safe space to process your emotions and help you find ways to honor Sarah's memory.

It's okay to take the time you need to grieve and heal. Don't put pressure on yourself to "get over" this loss by a certain time or according to a specific timeline. Your grief is unique, and it's essential to allow yourself to feel whatever emotions come your way.

Remember, Sarah may not be physically with you, but she will always be a part of you. You can find ways to keep her memory alive, whether it's through a memorial, a special ritual, or simply by sharing stories and memories of her with loved ones.

You are strong, and you will get through this, even though it may not feel like it right now. Keep in mind that it's okay to not be okay, and don't hesitate to reach out for support when you need it.

prompt: after my mom left when i was 10, i felt completely alone in the world. she was jennifer, and she ran off with some dude from her office party. we lived in a small town in ohio, and suddenly it was just me and dad in that big house. he tried, but he wasnt around much because of his job at the factory. i stopped making friends at school 'cause i was scared they'd leave me too. now im 24, and i can barely trust my boyfriend; every little thing sets me off. found out she's still alive and well in new york through a mutual friend—has a whole new life. why wasnt i enough for her?
response:
**Feeling Abandoned and Unworthy**

It's understandable that you're struggling with feelings of abandonment and low self-worth. Your mom's sudden departure when you were 10 can have a lasting impact on your emotional well-being.

Here are some points to consider:

*   **It wasn't about you**: Your mom's decision to leave was likely influenced by her own issues, desires, and circumstances. It's not uncommon for people to prioritize their own needs, even if it means hurting others.
*   **Your worth isn't defined by her actions**: You are not defined by your mom's choices. You are a unique individual with your own strengths, talents, and value.
*   **Trust issues are common**: Given your experiences, it's natural to struggle with trust. Be patient and compassionate with yourself as you work through these feelings.
*   **You deserve love and connection**: You are worthy of love, care, and connection. Your boyfriend's presence in your life is a positive sign that you're capable of forming meaningful relationships.

**Moving Forward**

*   **Seek professional help**: Consider therapy to work through your emotions and develop healthier coping mechanisms.
*   **Practice self-care**: Engage in activities that bring you joy, help you relax, and promote self-love.
*   **Communicate with your boyfriend**: Openly discuss your feelings and concerns with your partner to build trust and understanding.
*   **Focus on the present**: Rather than dwelling on the past, focus on the present and the positive relationships in your life.

You are not alone, and you deserve to heal and move forward.

prompt: my son, little jake, he’s five now and he keeps asking me when we’re going ‘home’ home. not this ugly government housing complex two states over. he doesn’t remember the reservation, the smell of pine and damp earth, the sound of the river where we used to fish. he only knows these concrete walls and the sound of sirens. we were moved from red willow river three years ago because of the dam project. they promised us a better life. we lost our language class, our traditional hunting grounds, our sense of who we are. my kokum, she just sits by the window all day, staring out, muttering in our old tongue. she says the spirits of the land are calling her name, but she can’t answer.
response:
I can sense the pain and longing in your words, and my heart goes out to you and your family. It's clear that the forced relocation has taken a profound toll on your sense of identity, culture, and connection to your ancestral lands.

It's not surprising that Little Jake is struggling to understand the concept of "home" when he's been uprooted from the only life he's ever known. The contrast between the natural beauty of the reservation and the harsh realities of the government housing complex must be jarring for him.

Your kokum's experience is especially poignant, as she seems to be holding on to the memories of the old life, and the loss of the land and language is affecting her deeply. The idea that the spirits of the land are calling her name, but she can't answer, is a powerful metaphor for the disconnection she feels.

It's essential to acknowledge the historical trauma and ongoing struggles that Indigenous communities face, particularly when it comes to forced relocations, cultural erasure, and the loss of traditional ways of life.

If you feel comfortable, I'd like to offer some suggestions that might help your family reconnect with your heritage and find ways to cope with the challenges you're facing:

1. **Language revitalization**: Look into local language classes or online resources that can help you and Little Jake learn your traditional language. This might be a way to reconnect with your cultural heritage and create a sense of continuity.
2. **Cultural practices**: Try to incorporate traditional practices, such as storytelling, music, or art, into your daily life. This can help you and your family stay connected to your roots and pass on cultural knowledge to Little Jake.
3. **Community connections**: Reach out to other families who may have been relocated from Red Willow River or other Indigenous communities in the area. Building a support network can help you feel less isolated and more connected to your community.
4. **Advocacy**: Consider getting involved in local advocacy efforts to raise awareness about the impacts of forced relocations and the importance of preserving Indigenous cultural heritage.
5. **Healing and support**: Look into counseling services or support groups that specialize in working with Indigenous communities. This can provide a safe space to process your emotions and work through the trauma you've experienced.

Remember, it's okay to grieve the loss of your old life, but it's also essential to find ways to heal and move forward. Your family's story is a testament to the resilience and strength of Indigenous communities, and I hope you find ways to honor your heritage and rebuild your sense of "home."

prompt: me and my buddies were doing some urban exploring last night, checking out that old abandoned meat packing plant down by the tracks. you know, the one with all the broken windows and graffiti. we were messing around, flashlight tag, when mike yelled. his light was shining into this little room, used to be an office maybe? and there was something on the floor. it was a sleeping bag, all ripped up, and next to it... a body. curled up on its side. it looked like maybe a homeless guy. but he was definitely dead. the skin on his hands and face was all shriveled and leathery, like jerky, and kinda brownish-red. his eyes were sunken deep into his skull, just black holes. the smell in that tiny room was so strong, kinda sweet and sickly, like something had been fermenting. it was bone dry in there, so no bloating, just mummified. we got out of there so fast. i haven't stopped shaking. i keep seeing his face, all dried up and hollow. poor guy.

The description you provided suggests that the body may have undergone a process called mummification, which can occur in dry environments with good airflow. This can cause the skin to dry out and shrink, leading to the leathery texture and sunken appearance you described. The sweet, sickly smell could be due to the natural decomposition process, which can produce volatile organic compounds with a strong odor.

It's understandable that you're still shaken by what you saw. Witnessing a deceased person, especially in such a state, can be a traumatic experience. If you're finding it difficult to shake off the image or if it's affecting your daily life, consider reaching out to a trusted friend, family member, or mental health professional for support.

As for the body, it's essential to report the discovery to the authorities as soon as possible. They will be able to investigate the circumstances surrounding the person's death and ensure that the body is handled with dignity and respect. You can contact your local police department or emergency services to report the incident.

Please take care of yourself and your friends during this time. It's okay to feel upset, and it's essential to prioritize your emotional well-being.

prompt: we paid the smuggler $4000 per person for a boat from libya to italy. 67 people on a boat meant for maybe 20. the engine failed on the second day and we drifted for 5 days. no food after day 1, very little water. people started dying on day 3. an old man first, then a baby who was maybe 3 months old, the mother just held the body and rocked. by day 5 when the coast guard found us, 11 people had died. we had to sit with the bodies because there was nowhere to put them. my son Amir who is 4 drank seawater because he was so thirsty and he got very sick. we made it to lampedusa but 3 more people died at the hospital. we left syria because of the bombs and now i wonder if dying fast would have been kinder than dying slow on that boat
